changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "There Are More Than Four Perpetrators?"

TextENGNew: ``Death by hemorrhagic shock from clawing open his own throat.@k@vS20/00/443200002. Skin fragments remain under the nails. They’re unmistakably his own.@k@vS20/00/443200003. The wound patterns match as well.``
changeReason: Multiple meaningful corrections: 'hypovolemic shock' to 'hemorrhagic shock' (matching JPN), 'tissue' to 'skin fragments' (JPN 皮膚片), and 'scars' to 'wound patterns' (JPN 傷の形).

TextENGNew: ``Yeah, yeah. I know the immediate cause of death was suicide.``
``I know what you’re after. You want to know if something could have artificially induced this kind of behavior, right?``
changeReason: Old asks if 'the cause of death is unnatural'; new correctly asks if 'something could have artificially induced this behavior,' matching the JPN about whether symptoms could be caused artificially.

TextENGNew: ``It’s not quite the same as scratching your back too hard and drawing a little blood, after all.``
Some of Tomitake’s fingernails had been torn off.
Fingernails actually come off fairly easily.
But it hurts terribly.
Which is why, under normal circumstances, no one would scratch hard enough to tear them off.
And then there were the numerous bruises on Tomitake’s body.
...From their shape and other characteristics, it was clear they were inflicted by bare-handed assault—and by multiple assailants at that.
changeReason: Old hedges with 'someone or a group'; new correctly states 'bare-handed assault by multiple assailants,' matching the JPN's certainty about multiple unarmed attackers.

TextENGNew: ``Judging from the secretions, there’s no doubt the deceased was in an extreme state of agitation.``
changeReason: Old says 'according to the toxicology report'; new correctly says 'judging from the secretions' (JPN 分泌物), a different evidentiary basis entirely.

TextENGNew: ``So he got into a brawl, and in his frenzy he clawed his own throat out? His attackers must have been stunned.``
Certainly, a person driven to extreme agitation in an extreme environment can behave in ways that a sound-minded individual would never imagine.
Of course, such cases are exceedingly rare.
``Actually, Ooishi. That wooden plank the deceased supposedly used as a weapon?@k@vS20/00/443200007. All they found on it were grains of sand and flecks of guardrail paint.``
``What about clothing fibers from the perp, or skin fragments?``
changeReason: Old omits 'from the perp' (JPN ホシの); new specifies 'clothing fibers from the perp,' clarifying whose evidence is being sought.

TextENGNew: ``Nothing. The deceased never struck his attackers.@k@vS20/00/443200009. ...Or perhaps the attackers took away the plank he actually hit them with.``
``If that were the case, they wouldn’t have gone out of their way to leave a different plank behind. They’d have taken them all.``
``Ha ha ha ha! Fair point.``
``Tomitake was pretty well built—tanned, good physique. ...He was obviously athletic.``
``Hm? That’s right.@k@vS20/00/443200012. Looks like he exercised regularly.``
...I couldn’t say what sport he practiced when he was alive, but his physical fitness was clearly above average.
In other words, he should have been no pushover in a fight.
A man with that kind of build, sensing mortal danger, swinging a weapon for all he’s worth—
it’s simply inconceivable that he wouldn’t land a single blow on his attackers.
Especially when his opponents were unarmed.
changeReason: Old uses singular 'opponent'; new correctly uses plural 'opponents,' consistent with the discussion about multiple attackers.

TextENGNew: He had a wooden plank, so you’d think he’d have hit them at least once...
``To surround and overpower someone with this kind of physique... how many people would it take, I wonder.``
``You dunce. That’s more your area of expertise.@k@vS20/00/443200014. Try remembering your old roughneck days!``
changeReason: Old translates 悪タレ時代 as 'Bad Cop days' implying a policing style; new correctly renders it as 'roughneck days,' referring to Ooishi's rough youth.

TextENGNew: If I were to take this guy in a fight, how many men would I want?
Just as a pack can bring down big game, attacking in numbers is fundamental when hunting larger prey.
...About four, I’d say.
Even accounting for differences in build, that many should be enough to manage.
``In that case, there were quite a few perpetrators.@k@vS20/00/443200016. So any group of four or more who got rowdy and drunk at the festival would be suspicious?``
...A group of four or more.
But... with that many people, couldn’t they have hidden the body somewhere less conspicuous?
Or... did he somehow manage to escape while on the brink of death...
If so, there would be no reason to commit suicide.
And beyond that, I still can’t fathom that grotesque manner of death...@kNothing but mysteries.
``I’m taking this very seriously on my end as well. I intend to be thorough, but... don’t get your hopes up.@k@vS20/00/443200018. After all, there’s no precedent for a death like this.``
``I’m not getting my hopes up. But I will be looking forward to the results.``
``Ooishi-san~! The chief is calling for you!``
``Excuse me, then. Let’s meet again next year.``
changeReason: Old 'Thanks. See ya round' drops the specific 'next year' timeframe; new correctly includes 'Let's meet again next year' (JPN また来年お会いしましょう).

TextENGNew: ``Sure thing. Have a good one!``
